title: Justice Chandru’s Unswerving Paths of Principle!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, omitted by OCR. The spelling `petiton` in Justice Chandru's English quotation is visibly printed in the source and is therefore preserved rather than treated as OCR damage. Other repairs are limited to unambiguous Tamil word joins and glyph errors. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: After retiring from the Madras High Court, Justice K. Chandru has periodically written articles about his legal experience. He compiled some of the important judgments he delivered while on the bench and published them under the excellent title *My Judgments in the Light of Ambedkar*. A few days ago, he sent me the book to read. Selected portions of it form today’s letter to my Udanpirappē. Should you not share the happiness I felt while reading it?
The book presents fifteen judgments. The titles alone will tell you who Justice Chandru is. Here are some of them:

1. Reservation 2. Inter-caste marriages 3. The wall of untouchability brought down 4. Dalit girls subjected to sexual violence 5. The Dalits’ right to life 6. Panchami land 7. Religious conversion 8. The harm that befell a library 9. The right to worship in temples

Under “Reservation,” Justice Chandru includes several of his judgments. In Tirunelveli district, the Collector appointed a woman named Arumugathai as a cooking assistant at a school’s nutritious-meal centre. Because she belonged to a Scheduled Caste, the school correspondent refused to let her join, claiming that the appointment had been made without consulting him. She complained to the Collector, who directed that she be admitted to duty and warned that otherwise the school would be closed. The correspondent filed a writ petition in the High Court. Justice Chandru dismissed it and ordered him to allow the woman to begin work immediately.

A rule governing appointments to nutritious-meal centres required applicants to reside within one kilometre of the centre. Because Scheduled Caste women lived in colonies outside their villages, they were unable to obtain these jobs. A woman named Vasantha challenged the rule. Justice Chandru held that the one-kilometre condition violated the Constitution. He added that where two candidates received equal marks in selection, the committee could give preference to the one living nearer the centre.

After it was claimed that reservation did not apply to posts in nutritious-meal centres, a woman named Pothumalli from Tiruvarur district brought a case. Justice Chandru ordered that the reservation roster must be followed whenever those posts were filled.

Describing the judgment, he also wrote openly about me: “The then Chief Minister Kalaignar Karunanidhi immediately implemented this judgment and issued Government Order No. 142 through the Social Welfare Department on 6-7-2010. Through that order, nearly twenty-five thousand Dalit women gained an opportunity to obtain nutritious-meal employment through reservation.”

Across the fifteen subjects, Justice Chandru describes his judgments and connects each to Ambedkar’s philosophy, thereby justifying the book’s title, *My Judgments in the Light of Ambedkar*. Reading it, I understood that Ambedkar’s ideas were consonant with those of Thanthai Periyar and Perarignar Anna, and that Justice Chandru delivered these judgments on that foundation.
One article is titled “Inter-caste Marriages.” It quotes the Supreme Court on cruelties committed in society against such marriages:

“There is no dispute that the petitioner is an adult and was an adult at the relevant time. She therefore has the freedom to marry anyone she wishes or live with the person of her choice. Neither the Hindu Marriage Act nor any other law prohibits inter-caste marriage.

“We find that the accused, the inter-caste couple, committed no offence. The criminal case, instigated because the brothers were angered that their sister married outside the caste, is a complete abuse of judicial process and the administrative machinery. It greatly pains us that instead of acting against the unlawful and oppressive conduct of those brothers, the police acted against the woman’s husband and his relatives.

“Numerous incidents of harassment, intimidation and violence against young men and women who marry outside their caste have come to our notice, and we therefore make these general observations. At a time when the country is passing through a difficult period in its history, the court cannot remain silent in matters of public interest such as this.

“The caste system is a curse upon the country. The sooner it is destroyed, the better. Inter-caste marriages are in the national interest because they help abolish caste. This is a free democratic country. Once a person becomes an adult, he or she may marry anyone of their choice.

“No person entering an inter-caste or inter-religious marriage should be intimidated, subjected to violence, incited against or harassed. We direct administrative and police authorities throughout the country to ensure that couples in inter-caste and inter-religious marriages are not harassed, intimidated or subjected to violence. If anyone engages in, or instigates, intimidation, harassment or violence, criminal cases must be registered and strict action taken under the law.”

Justice Chandru cited this stern Supreme Court declaration in connection with one of his own judgments. A person who helped an inter-caste couple was denied a police job because a criminal case filed against him remained pending.

Sivanesan’s friend Sudhakar and Arachelvi, who had studied with Sudhakar, entered an inter-caste love marriage. Sivanesan went as a witness to register the marriage. Arachelvi’s sister-in-law was a sub-inspector in the local police. Through her efforts, a false case was registered and both Sudhakar and Sivanesan were imprisoned.

While the case remained pending, Sivanesan was selected as a Grade II police constable. The background report mentioned the case, and he was denied the post. He filed a writ petition. When it came before Justice Chandru, the judge cited the Supreme Court’s observations on inter-caste marriage and held that denying Sivanesan employment was wrong.
This article attracted me because its view of inter-caste marriage accords with the ideas long advanced by Thanthai Periyar, Perarignar Anna, the Dravidar Kazhagam and the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam.

Under the title “Relief Denied to Policemen Who Committed Atrocities,” Justice Chandru discusses two or three judgments. In 1995, police and forest officials entered Vachathi, an Adivasi village in Dharmapuri district, brutally assaulted its people and raped several women. A subsequent inquiry commission headed by Justice Banumathi found supporting evidence and recommended criminal cases and departmental proceedings against the officials. The case was entrusted to the CBI.
Twelve policemen petitioned the High Court to prevent departmental proceedings before the criminal case concluded. Justice Chandru dismissed their petitions, writing:

“Considering that the petitioners were charged for grave offences such as raping tribal women, the question of stalling the departmental action cannot indefinitely be postponed. In the light of the above factual matrix and the legal precedents cited above, this writ petiton stands dismissed.”

The book also presents his ruling in another case. On the night of 22-11-2011, four Dalit women were returning from work along the Manampoondi bypass in Tirukkoyilur taluk. An inspector from Tirukkoyilur police station, a special sub-inspector, a head constable and two constables who were patrolling there allegedly overpowered and raped them. Following the women’s complaint, the Deputy Inspector General for the Villupuram range suspended the officers. They filed writ petitions in the High Court seeking cancellation of their suspensions. Justice Chandru dismissed those petitions.

I read the book in full. It contains many of Justice Chandru’s judgments, especially those concerning cases brought against Dalit people. I have included these matters in this letter only because I wanted you too to know his views, and for no other hidden purpose.
Some will say that a judge who delivered such judgments also criticised the DMK government and even me. Even so, I have written this in appreciation of the judgments he records in the book.
With affection,
M.K.
